name: Paris
visaSummary: Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements well in advance; a Schengen visa issued by any member state covers France.
bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October offer mild weather, blooming parks and thinner crowds than peak summer.
weatherSummary: Temperate oceanic climate with mild, damp winters (highs 7-8°C) and warm summers (highs 24-25°C); rain is possible year-round so pack a compact umbrella regardless of season.
etiquette: Always greet shopkeepers and waitstaff with "Bonjour" before starting a conversation, even in English.
Keep your voice down on public transport and in restaurants — loud conversation is considered impolite.
Dress reasonably smart; athleisure and flip-flops mark tourists and can draw less attentive service.
Tipping is not mandatory since service is included (service compris), but rounding up or leaving small change for good service is appreciated.
Say "Bonjour" and "Merci, au revoir" when entering and leaving small shops — silence is read as rude.

A compact umbrella for year-round rain
A smart-casual outfit for nicer restaurants

travelTips: Buy Louvre and Versailles tickets online in advance to skip long queue times.
Many museums are free on the first Sunday of the month from October to March.
Avoid dining right on the Champs-Élysées — walk a few streets over for better value and quality.
The Paris Museum Pass can save money if visiting 3+ major sites in a few days.
Metro line 1 and RER B (airport line) are known pickpocket hotspots — keep bags zipped and in front.
thingsToAvoid: The "gold ring" and "friendship bracelet" scams near Sacré-Cœur and the Eiffel Tower.
Unofficial ticket sellers outside major attractions — buy only from official websites.
Petition-signature scams (often a distraction for pickpocketing) near tourist landmarks.
Restaurants directly facing major monuments — usually overpriced and mediocre.
Walking distracted with a phone out near Châtelet-Les Halles or Barbès at night.

name: London
visaSummary: US, Canadian, Australian, New Zealand and most EU passport holders can visit visa-free for up to 6 months as a Standard Visitor. As of 2025, most non-visa-national travellers (including EU citizens) require an Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) applied for online before arrival.
bestTimeToVisit: Late March to May and September to October offer pleasant temperatures, blooming parks and fewer crowds than peak summer.
weatherSummary: Temperate maritime climate with mild, wet winters (highs 8°C) and mild summers (highs 23°C); rain and overcast skies are possible any month.
etiquette: Queueing is taken seriously — always join the back of a line and never cut in.
Stand on the right on Underground escalators to let people pass on the left.
A polite "sorry" and "please/thank you" go a long way, even in minor interactions.
Tipping around 10-12.5% is customary in restaurants if service isn't already included.
Avoid discussing salary or being overly loud in public spaces — reserved conversation is the norm.

localTransportGuide: The Underground ("the Tube") is the fastest way around; use a contactless card or Oyster card and tap in/out for the best fares. Black cabs and Uber are plentiful, and the Elizabeth line offers quick cross-city and Heathrow connections.
packingNotes: A compact umbrella or rain jacket for unpredictable weather
Layered clothing for cool mornings and mild afternoons
Comfortable shoes for extensive walking
A smart-casual outfit for theatre or upscale dining
travelTips: Book West End theatre tickets in advance or check day-of "TKTS" booth deals in Leicester Square.
Use contactless payment or an Oyster card on the Tube — it automatically caps your daily fare.
Many major museums are free, so budget your money for special exhibitions instead.
Sunday trading hours are shorter for many shops, typically 11am-5pm.
Book Harry Potter Studio Tour tickets weeks ahead — it regularly sells out.
thingsToAvoid: Fake "charity worker" clipboard scams around tourist areas like Oxford Street.
Unofficial minicabs touting outside nightclubs — use licensed black cabs or ride apps.
Standing on the left side of Underground escalators — it blocks foot traffic.
Overpaying for meals in extremely touristy zones like directly outside Buckingham Palace.
Traveling during rush hour (8-9:30am, 5-6:30pm) with luggage — Tube carriages get packed.

name: Rome
visaSummary: Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October bring warm, pleasant weather and thinner crowds than the peak summer heat.
weatherSummary: Mediterranean climate with mild, rainy winters (highs 12°C) and hot, dry summers (highs 31°C); August can be uncomfortably hot for walking tours.
etiquette: Dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered) when visiting churches and the Vatican.
Cappuccino is a breakfast drink — ordering one after 11am or with a meal marks you as a tourist.
Don't sit on monument steps or fountains where signs prohibit it — fines are enforced.
Greet with "Buongiorno" or "Buonasera" when entering shops and restaurants.
Tipping is not obligatory; rounding up or leaving a small amount for good service is fine.

travelTips: Book Colosseum and Vatican Museum tickets online well ahead to skip multi-hour queues.
Fiumicino Airport's Leonardo Express train runs directly to Termini Station in 32 minutes.
Many top sites like the Pantheon and Trevi Fountain are free — budget for skip-the-line tours instead.
Restaurants near major landmarks are often tourist traps — walk a few blocks for better, cheaper food.
Validate paper transit tickets before boarding buses/trams to avoid fines.
thingsToAvoid: Unofficial "gladiators" charging for photos near the Colosseum — tips are expected and often excessive.
Restaurants with picture-menus and aggressive touts right by tourist sites.
Fake or scalped skip-the-line tickets sold by street vendors — buy only from official sources.
Ordering cappuccino after a meal — locals only drink it in the morning.
Pickpockets on crowded routes like Bus 64 (Termini to Vatican).

name: Barcelona
visaSummary: Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: May, June and September offer warm, sunny weather with more manageable crowds than July-August peak season.
weatherSummary: Mediterranean climate with mild winters (highs 14°C) and hot, humid summers (highs 29°C); coastal breezes make it more comfortable than inland Spain.
etiquette: Lunch is typically eaten 2-3pm and dinner rarely before 9pm — restaurants may be empty earlier.
A brief "Hola" and "Gràcies"/"Gracias" when entering and leaving shops is polite.
Beachwear should stay at the beach — cover up when walking through city streets.
Catalan pride runs deep; acknowledging the region's distinct culture and language is appreciated.
Siesta-era shop closures (roughly 2-5pm) are less common now but still happen in smaller stores.

localTransportGuide: The metro (TMB) is efficient and covers most tourist areas — a T-Casual 10-trip card offers good value. The Aerobus connects El Prat Airport to the city center in about 35 minutes, and Bicing bike-share or walking work well within the compact old town.

Comfortable shoes for hilly Gaudí sites
A light jacket for cooler evenings

travelTips: Book Sagrada Família tickets weeks in advance — it frequently sells out even in shoulder season.
Watch your belongings closely on La Rambla and the metro, both known pickpocket zones.
Many tapas bars charge more for seated table service than standing at the bar.
The Hola BCN travel card bundles unlimited transit for set periods, useful for longer stays.
Dinner reservations before 8:30pm may find you eating alone — locals dine late.
thingsToAvoid: Fake "petition" or bracelet distraction scams near Sagrada Família and La Rambla.
Unlicensed street vendors selling counterfeit goods — police crackdowns can implicate buyers too.
Leaving bags unattended at beach or outdoor café tables.
Overpriced sangria-and-paella combo menus advertised on La Rambla — quality is usually poor.
Swimming at Barceloneta Beach late at night — theft from unattended belongings is common.

name: Amsterdam
visaSummary: Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: April (tulip season) through May, and September, offer mild weather, blooming Keukenhof gardens and moderate crowds.
weatherSummary: Temperate maritime climate with cool, wet winters (highs 6°C) and mild summers (highs 21°C); wind and rain are common year-round, so layering is key.
etiquette: Cyclists have the right of way in bike lanes — always look before stepping into one.
The Dutch are direct communicators; blunt feedback is normal, not rude.
Cannabis use is tolerated only in licensed coffeeshops, not openly on the street.
Splitting the bill ("going Dutch") is standard practice among friends and colleagues.
Greet with a simple nod or handshake; overt formality isn't expected.

localTransportGuide: Renting a bicycle is the most authentic way to get around flat, compact Amsterdam — dedicated bike lanes cover the whole city. Trams and the GVB public transit network (use an OV-chipkaart or contactless card) fill in the rest, and the center is very walkable.
packingNotes: A waterproof jacket for frequent rain
Warm layers even in summer evenings
Comfortable shoes for cobblestones
A bike-friendly bag if planning to cycle
travelTips: Book Anne Frank House tickets exactly 6 weeks ahead online — they sell out almost immediately.
Rent a bike from a reputable shop and always lock both wheels — bike theft is common.
The I Amsterdam City Card bundles free museum entry and transit for short stays.
Coffeeshops sell cannabis; cafés serve regular coffee — don't confuse the two when ordering.
Visit the Nine Streets on weekday mornings to avoid weekend shopping crowds.
thingsToAvoid: Walking or standing in marked bike lanes — cyclists move fast and won't always stop.
Photographing sex workers in the Red Light District — it's illegal and disrespectful.
Buying drugs from street dealers — only licensed coffeeshops are legal and safe.
Renting bikes without proper locks in high-theft areas like Centraal Station.
Overpaying at central-canal restaurants — better value is a short walk into residential streets.

name: Santorini
visaSummary: Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: Late April to June and September to early October give warm weather, calm seas and lighter crowds than the packed July-August peak.
weatherSummary: Mediterranean island climate with mild, breezy winters (highs 15°C) and hot, dry summers (highs 29°C); strong winds (the "meltemi") are common in summer.
etiquette: Modest dress is expected when entering Orthodox churches — cover shoulders and knees.
Sunset viewing spots in Oia get extremely crowded; arrive early and be patient with space.
Tipping around 5-10% in restaurants is appreciated though not mandatory.
Many businesses close for a midday rest in shoulder season; plan around siesta hours.
Respect private property — many caldera-view spots are on hotel land, not public paths.

localTransportGuide: Renting an ATV or small car is the most flexible way to explore beyond Fira and Oia, since the island is hilly and bus routes are limited. Local KTEL buses run between main villages, and taxis are scarce and should be booked ahead for airport or ferry transfers.
packingNotes: Comfortable, grippy shoes for steep cobbled caldera steps
Sun protection — hat, high-SPF sunscreen, sunglasses
A light wind layer for breezy clifftop evenings
Swimwear plus a cover-up for beach-to-town transitions
travelTips: Book caldera-view accommodation in Oia or Imerovigli months ahead for peak season.
Arrive at the Oia sunset viewpoint at least an hour early in summer to get a spot.
Ferries between islands can be cancelled due to wind — build buffer days into island-hopping itineraries.
Red and black sand beaches get scorching hot midday — bring sandals for the walk to the water.
Renting an ATV is cheaper and more practical than taxis for reaching multiple villages in a day.
thingsToAvoid: Overpaying at restaurants directly on the Oia sunset strip — quality doesn't always match the price.
Skipping travel insurance — ferry and flight delays due to weather are common.
Wandering onto caldera-view hotel terraces for photos — these are private property.
Driving rental ATVs without a valid international license — checks do happen.
Assuming all beaches are sandy — several are pebble or volcanic rock, uncomfortable barefoot.

name: Venice
visaSummary: Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October bring mild, pleasant weather while avoiding the worst summer crowds and heat.
weatherSummary: Humid subtropical climate with cool, misty winters (highs 8°C, occasional acqua alta flooding) and warm, humid summers (highs 28°C).
etiquette: Dress modestly (covered shoulders/knees) when entering St. Mark's Basilica.
Avoid sitting on church steps or bridges to eat — some areas fine for littering/loitering.
Address gondoliers and boat staff politely; haggling over quoted gondola prices is unusual.
Keep to the right when walking in narrow calli (alleys) to let foot traffic flow.
A day-tripper tourist tax applies for some visitors — check current entry requirements before arrival.

localTransportGuide: Venice has no cars — get around on foot or via the ACTV vaporetto (water bus) network, with multi-day passes offering good value for island hopping. Water taxis are fast but expensive; gondolas are for scenic leisure rather than practical transport.
packingNotes: Waterproof shoes for possible acqua alta flooding (autumn/winter)
Modest attire for basilica visits
A refillable water bottle for public fountains
Comfortable shoes for extensive bridge-and-alley walking
travelTips: Book Doge's Palace and St. Mark's Basilica tickets online to skip long lines.
Get lost intentionally in quieter Cannaregio and Dorsoduro to escape San Marco crowds.
Check the tide forecast in autumn/winter — acqua alta can flood St. Mark's Square.
Eat where locals eat: bacari (wine bars) serving cicchetti are cheaper and more authentic than sit-down tourist restaurants.
Venice charges a day-tripper access fee on certain peak dates — check requirements before visiting.
thingsToAvoid: Unlicensed gondoliers or "helpers" who approach with unsolicited offers — book through official stands.
Feeding pigeons in St. Mark's Square — it's prohibited and fined.
Buying "designer" bags from street vendors — counterfeits carry legal risk for buyers too.
Restaurants with tourist-menu photos directly off St. Mark's Square — overpriced and low quality.
Wheeled suitcases over Venice's many small bridges — consider a backpack instead.

bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October bring warm days, blooming Tuscan countryside and fewer crowds than midsummer.
weatherSummary: Mediterranean climate with cool, damp winters (highs 11°C) and hot, humid summers (highs 32°C) that can feel stifling in the enclosed city center.
etiquette: Dress modestly for the Duomo and other churches — shoulders and knees covered.
Order gelato from artisanal ("gelateria artigianale") shops rather than garish, mounded displays aimed at tourists.
Greet shopkeepers with "Buongiorno/Buonasera" before browsing.
Eating on the steps of the Duomo or monuments is often discouraged/fined.
Standing at a café bar for espresso is cheaper than table service — check pricing signs.

localTransportGuide: The historic center is compact and best explored on foot — most major sights are within a 20-minute walk of the Duomo. ATAF buses cover areas outside the walkable core, and trains from Santa Maria Novella station make easy day trips to Pisa, Siena and Lucca.
packingNotes: Modest clothing for church and Duomo visits
Comfortable shoes for cobblestones and hill climbs
A light layer for cool museum interiors
Sun protection for summer piazza walking
travelTips: Book Uffizi and Accademia tickets online well ahead — same-day lines can stretch for hours.
Climb Giotto's Campanile instead of the Duomo dome if the dome is sold out — similar views, less crowded.
Visit Piazzale Michelangelo at sunset but arrive by public bus 12 or 13 to skip the uphill walk.
Try schiacciata (Tuscan flatbread sandwiches) at All'Antico Vinaio for an affordable, iconic lunch.
Combine a Florence base with day trips by train to Pisa or Siena rather than moving hotels.
thingsToAvoid: Leather stalls at San Lorenzo Market claiming "genuine leather" without verification — quality varies widely.
Restaurants with multilingual tourist menus directly around the Duomo — overpriced, average food.
Touching or leaning on Renaissance artwork/sculptures — heavy fines apply.
Visiting the Uffizi without a reservation in peak season — queues can exceed 3 hours.
Cheap "gelato" with unnaturally bright colors and tall mounds — a sign of low-quality artificial ingredients.

name: Swiss Alps
visaSummary: Switzerland is part of the Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: June to September for hiking, alpine lakes and cable-car access to high trails; December to March for skiing in Zermatt, Interlaken and Jungfrau resorts.
weatherSummary: Alpine climate that varies sharply with altitude — valley towns like Interlaken see mild summers (highs 23°C) and cold, snowy winters (highs 2°C), while high peaks stay snow-covered year-round.
etiquette: Punctuality is highly valued — trains and appointments run precisely on schedule.
Greet with a firm handshake and formal address until invited to use first names.
Keep noise down in residential areas, especially on Sundays when many activities (like mowing lawns) are discouraged.
Recycling and waste sorting are taken seriously — follow posted bin instructions at accommodations.
Hiking trail etiquette: yield to uphill walkers and close gates behind you in farmland.

localTransportGuide: The Swiss rail network is punctual and scenic — a Swiss Travel Pass covers trains, buses, boats and many cable cars region-wide. Zermatt itself is car-free, reached by a connecting train, and postbuses fill in mountain villages not served by rail.
packingNotes: Layered clothing for sharp temperature swings between valley and summit
Sturdy hiking boots for alpine trails
Sunglasses and high-SPF sunscreen (UV is stronger at altitude)
A warm jacket even in summer for cable-car summits
travelTips: Buy Jungfraujoch and Gornergrat tickets in the morning for the clearest mountain visibility.
A Swiss Travel Pass often pays for itself with 2-3 train/cable-car excursions.
Check webcams for summit weather before paying for high-altitude excursions — clouds can obscure views.
Book mountain restaurants or huts in advance during peak hiking season (July-August).
ATMs and cards are widely accepted, but small mountain kiosks may prefer cash (CHF).
thingsToAvoid: Underestimating alpine weather — conditions can change from sun to snow within an hour at altitude.
Skipping travel insurance with mountain rescue coverage for hiking/skiing trips.
Assuming euros are accepted everywhere — Switzerland uses Swiss francs, and euro change-back rates are poor.
Hiking marked trails without proper footwear — steep alpine paths can be slippery.
Booking same-day Jungfraujoch tickets in peak season without checking capacity — trains can sell out.

name: Prague

bestTimeToVisit: April to May and September to October offer mild weather and thinner crowds than the packed summer months.
weatherSummary: Continental climate with cold winters (highs 2°C, occasional snow) and warm summers (highs 24°C); spring and autumn are mild and pleasant.
etiquette: Greet with "Dobrý den" when entering shops or restaurants.
Tipping around 10% is customary in restaurants; round up for smaller purchases.
Remove shoes when entering a Czech home, if invited.
Avoid loud, rowdy behavior — Prague has cracked down on stag/hen party excesses in the historic center.
Don't jaywalk across tram tracks — trams have priority and stop distances are long.

A warm coat for winter (highs near freezing)
Layers for unpredictable spring/autumn weather

travelTips: Buy a 24 or 72-hour transit pass if planning to use trams/metro frequently.
Climb Petřín or Vyšehrad instead of the crowded Castle viewpoint for similar views with fewer people.
Czech beer is famously cheap and high-quality — often less than bottled water at restaurants.
Exchange currency at bank-affiliated kiosks, not the "0% commission" storefronts near Old Town Square which use poor rates.
Visit Prague Castle early morning to beat tour bus crowds.
thingsToAvoid: Currency exchange booths advertising "0% commission" near tourist zones — rates are typically terrible.
Restaurants near Old Town Square with unmarked prices or no printed menu — check the bill for added charges.
Overpriced "traditional" Trdelník pastry stalls, which are actually more Slovak/Hungarian in origin.
Taxis hailed on the street without a metered app — use Bolt or Uber instead.
Walking on tram tracks or crossing without checking both directions.

etiquette: Formal titles and "Sie" (formal "you") are used until invited to switch to informal address.
Dress smartly for the opera, concerts and upscale coffeehouses.
Don't rush a coffeehouse visit — it's customary to linger with a single coffee for hours.
Jaywalking is frowned upon and can be fined even with no traffic present.
Punctuality for appointments and reservations is expected and respected.

A warm coat for cold winters

A Vienna City Card bundles transit with discounts to major museums.
Try a classic Viennese coffeehouse (Café Central, Hawelka) — lingering for hours is normal, not rude.

Dressing too casually for the Opera or fine-dining restaurants — smart attire is expected.

name: Lisbon

weatherSummary: Mediterranean climate with mild, rainy winters (highs 15°C) and hot, dry summers (highs 29°C), moderated by Atlantic breezes.
etiquette: A relaxed pace of life is the norm — meals and service are unhurried.
Greet with "Bom dia/Boa tarde" and a light kiss on both cheeks among friends (not strangers).
Tipping around 5-10% is appreciated but not obligatory.
Avoid comparing Portugal to Spain — locals take pride in a distinct identity and language.
Dress modestly when visiting churches and monasteries.

name: Reykjavik
visaSummary: Iceland is part of the Schengen area — visa-free entry for US, UK, Canadian, Australian and EU passport holders for stays up to 90 days within a 180-day period. Other nationalities should check Schengen short-stay visa requirements in advance.
bestTimeToVisit: June to August for the midnight sun, hiking and puffin season; late September to March for aurora borealis viewing (best clarity typically October and February-March).
weatherSummary: Subpolar oceanic climate, milder than the latitude suggests due to the Gulf Stream — cool summers (highs 14°C) and cold, windy winters (highs 2°C) with limited daylight.
etiquette: Always remove shoes before entering a home, and shower before entering public geothermal pools.
Icelanders are informal — first names are used with almost everyone, including strangers.
Never walk off-trail on moss-covered lava fields; the moss takes decades to regrow if damaged.
Tipping is not expected or customary anywhere in Iceland.
Respect closures at natural sites during high winds or ice — safety warnings are taken seriously here.

Rent a 4x4 in winter if planning to drive outside Reykjavik — roads can be icy and conditions change fast.

Tap water is safe, delicious and free — skip bottled water entirely.
Fuel up and stock snacks before long countryside drives — gas stations are sparse outside the capital.

Ask permission before photographing local residents, especially in small villages.
Bargaining is expected and welcomed in bazaars, but stay respectful and good-humored.
Remove shoes before entering mosques and some cave-hotel common areas.
It's polite to accept offered Turkish tea (çay) from shopkeepers, even briefly, as a gesture of hospitality.

Sunrise and sunset at Rose Valley or Sunset Point are free alternatives to paid viewpoints.
thingsToAvoid: Booking the cheapest balloon operator without checking safety records and reviews — quality varies significantly.
Wandering underground cities without a guide — passages are a maze and poorly lit in places.
Rug/carpet shops with high-pressure sales tactics — never feel obligated to buy after a "free" tea.
Underestimating temperature swings — cave rooms can be cold overnight even after hot days.
Driving valley roads at night without local knowledge — unlit, winding, and easy to get lost.

Bargaining is standard practice in the Grand Bazaar and Spice Bazaar — start well below asking price.
Accept offered Turkish tea graciously, even in shops, as declining outright can seem impolite.

Visiting the Grand Bazaar on Sundays when many sections are closed.

etiquette: Dress modestly, especially away from resort pools — shoulders and knees covered is respectful, particularly for women.
Ask before photographing people, especially in the souks and Jemaa el-Fnaa performers.
Bargaining is essential and expected in the souks — never accept the first price.
Use your right hand for eating, giving or receiving items.
During Ramadan, avoid eating, drinking or smoking in public during daylight hours out of respect.

Walking through the souks with valuables in easily accessible pockets — pickpocketing occurs in crowded lanes.

localTransportGuide: The Dubai Metro is modern, driverless and air-conditioned, connecting most major attractions along two lines with a Nol card for payment. Taxis and ride-hailing apps (Careem, Uber) are affordable and widely used for areas the metro doesn't reach, like Palm Jumeirah.
packingNotes: Modest clothing for malls and public areas outside beach/pool zones
Light, breathable fabrics for extreme heat
A light jacket for heavily air-conditioned malls and restaurants
Sun protection for desert and beach excursions
travelTips: Visit outdoor attractions and do desert safaris between November and March to avoid extreme summer heat.
Book Burj Khalifa "At the Top" tickets for sunset slots — they sell out and offer the best views.
Malls double as social hubs with free entertainment (fountain shows, ice rinks) — budget time to explore them.
Alcohol is only available in licensed hotel bars/restaurants, not supermarkets, so plan accordingly.
Friday is the start of the weekend in the UAE — some government offices and services close.
thingsToAvoid: Public displays of affection — even hand-holding beyond light contact can draw unwanted attention.
Photographing government buildings, military installations, or people without consent.
Drinking alcohol outside licensed venues, or being visibly intoxicated in public — both carry legal risk.
Overpacking the summer months with extensive outdoor plans — the heat can be genuinely dangerous.
Assuming all beaches are free — some upscale beach clubs charge a day-pass entry fee.

name: Maldives
visaSummary: Visa-free entry on arrival for all nationalities for tourist stays (typically up to 30 days), provided travelers have a confirmed hotel/resort booking, sufficient funds and a return ticket. No advance visa application is needed for most visitors.
bestTimeToVisit: December to April (dry season) offers the best combination of sunshine, calm seas and excellent diving visibility; it overlaps with the busiest and most expensive period.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate that stays warm and humid year-round (highs 30-31°C); May to November brings the monsoon season with more rain and wind, though showers are often brief.
etiquette: Dress modestly on local inhabited islands — swimwear is reserved for resort islands and designated bikini beaches.
Alcohol is only served on resort islands, not on local islands, in line with Islamic law.
Remove shoes before entering mosques, and dress conservatively if visiting one.
Public displays of affection should be minimal, especially on local islands.
Respect marine life — never touch coral or stand on reefs while snorkeling or diving.

localTransportGuide: Getting between islands means either a speedboat transfer (shorter distances) or a scenic seaplane (further atolls), both typically arranged directly through your resort. Within resort islands, transport is by foot or bicycle since most are small enough to cross in 10-15 minutes.
packingNotes: Reef-safe sunscreen (many resorts require it to protect coral)
Modest cover-up for transiting through Malé or visiting local islands
Snorkel gear if you have your own preferred fit
Light, breathable clothing — it is hot and humid year-round
travelTips: Book seaplane transfers as early as possible — daylight-only operating hours can create scheduling bottlenecks in peak season.
Confirm whether your resort island allows alcohol and swimwear-only zones since rules differ from local islands.
Pack reef-safe sunscreen; some resorts ban oxybenzone-based sunscreen to protect the coral.
Budget travelers can stay on local islands with guesthouses for a fraction of resort prices, using public ferries.
May-November monsoon season still has plenty of sun — showers are typically short and rates are notably lower.
thingsToAvoid: Bringing alcohol, pork products, or overtly religious materials of other faiths into the country — customs restrictions apply.
Standing on or touching coral reefs while snorkeling — irreversible damage occurs quickly.
Wearing swimwear on local, inhabited islands outside of designated bikini beaches.
Booking a resort without confirming seaplane vs speedboat transfer time/cost — some remote atolls add significant transfer expense.
Assuming all resorts include meals — many operate on room-only or bed-and-breakfast plans with costly à la carte dining.

name: Bali
visaSummary: Most nationalities including the US, UK, Canada, Australia and EU citizens can obtain a Visa on Arrival (typically valid 30 days, extendable once) for a fee paid on entry. Verify current requirements against your specific passport before travel.
bestTimeToVisit: April to October (dry season) offers sunny days and lower humidity, ideal for beaches, hiking and temple visits; July-August is peak crowds and pricing.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with warm temperatures year-round (highs 29-32°C); wet season runs roughly November to March with afternoon downpours, dry season April to October is sunnier.
etiquette: Dress modestly and wear a sarong/sash when entering Hindu temples.
Never touch someone's head, including children's — it's considered the most sacred body part.
Use your right hand for giving, receiving, and eating.
Step around, not over, offerings (canang sari) placed on the ground.
Remove shoes before entering homes and some temple areas.

localTransportGuide: Renting a scooter is the most common way to get around Bali's traffic, though hiring a private driver for the day is a safer, affordable option for longer trips between regions. Ride-hailing apps like Gojek and Grab work well in southern tourist hubs but are restricted in some traditional areas.
packingNotes: A sarong for temple visits (some temples provide one, but bringing your own helps)
Reef-safe sunscreen and rash guard for surfing/snorkeling
Light, breathable clothing for humidity
A light rain jacket for wet season showers
travelTips: Hire a private driver for full-day sightseeing — it's affordable and removes the stress of navigating chaotic traffic.
Visit Tegallalang and Lempuyang early morning to avoid both crowds and midday heat.
Carry a stash of small IDR bills for temple donations, parking and street food.
Check Nyepi (Day of Silence) dates if traveling in March — the entire island shuts down, including the airport, for 24 hours.
Negotiate scooter/car rental prices and always wear a helmet — traffic enforcement and accident risk are real.
thingsToAvoid: Renting a scooter without prior riding experience — Bali's traffic is chaotic and accidents among tourists are common.
Drinking tap water — stick to bottled or filtered water throughout the island.
Disrespecting temple dress codes — sarongs are mandatory at most sites, not optional.
The "closed road/temple" taxi scam redirecting you to a commission-paying shop — verify independently.
Swimming at Kuta or Uluwatu beaches during high surf without checking flag warnings — currents can be dangerous.

name: Bangkok
visaSummary: Most Western nationalities including the US, UK, Canada, Australia and EU citizens can enter visa-free for tourism (typically up to 30-60 days depending on nationality and entry method). Verify current requirements against your specific passport before travel.
bestTimeToVisit: November to February (cool, dry season) offers the most comfortable temperatures and humidity for exploring temples and street markets.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate, hot year-round with highs 31-35°C; the hot season (March-May) is intense, the rainy season (June-October) brings frequent afternoon downpours, and November-February is the most pleasant period.
etiquette: Never touch anyone's head or point your feet at people, monks, or Buddha images — both are considered highly disrespectful.
Dress modestly with shoulders and knees covered when visiting temples like the Grand Palace.
The wai (palms-together bow) is the traditional greeting; a slight bow back is polite when received.
Remove shoes before entering temples and many homes.
The monarchy is deeply revered — avoid any critical remarks about the royal family, which carries legal consequences.

localTransportGuide: The BTS Skytrain and MRT subway efficiently bypass Bangkok's notorious traffic for most tourist areas — a Rabbit Card makes fare payment seamless. Tuk-tuks and metered taxis (or Grab app) fill in the gaps, and the Chao Phraya Express boat is a scenic, practical way to reach riverside temples.
packingNotes: Modest clothing with shoulders/knees covered for temple visits
Lightweight, breathable fabrics for intense heat and humidity
A reusable water bottle (tap water isn't potable)
A light rain jacket during the June-October wet season
travelTips: Use the BTS/MRT for most journeys — Bangkok traffic can turn a short taxi ride into an hour-long ordeal.
Insist on the meter in taxis, or use the Grab app to avoid price haggling.
Dress appropriately for the Grand Palace — rental sarongs are available on-site if needed but queues form.
Try street food from stalls with high turnover and visible cooking — it's some of the best food in the city and generally very safe.
Avoid single-use tuk-tuk "sightseeing" offers quoting suspiciously low prices — they usually detour to gem/tailor shops for commission.
thingsToAvoid: The "Grand Palace is closed today" tuk-tuk scam — it's virtually always open; verify independently and ignore unsolicited claims.
Gem and tailor shop scams pitched by friendly strangers or tuk-tuk drivers offering suspiciously cheap tours.
Disrespecting Buddha imagery — never climb on statues or point your feet toward them for photos.
Riverside restaurants with aggressive street touts and no posted menu prices.
Drinking tap water or using it to brush teeth — stick to bottled or filtered water.

name: Phuket
visaSummary: Most Western nationalities including the US, UK, Canada, Australia and EU citizens can enter visa-free for tourism (typically up to 30-60 days depending on nationality and entry method). Verify current requirements against your specific passport before travel.
bestTimeToVisit: November to April (dry season) offers sunny skies and calm Andaman Sea waters, ideal for beaches and island-hopping.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with warm temperatures year-round (highs 31-33°C); the monsoon season (May-October) brings heavier rain and rougher seas, sometimes affecting boat trips.
etiquette: Dress modestly when visiting temples — shoulders and knees covered.
Never point your feet at people or Buddha images, and don't touch anyone's head.
Topless/nude sunbathing is not culturally accepted on Thai beaches.
Bargain politely and with a smile in markets — aggressive haggling is considered rude.
Respect flag warnings on beaches during monsoon season — rip currents cause fatalities most years.

localTransportGuide: Renting a scooter is common for short trips between beaches, but songthaews (shared pickup trucks) and Grab taxis are safer options for those unfamiliar with Thai traffic patterns. Longer island tours are almost always arranged as pre-booked speedboat excursions from your resort area.
packingNotes: Reef-safe sunscreen for snorkeling trips
A rash guard/wetsuit top for extended sun exposure in water
Light, quick-dry clothing for the humid tropical climate
A dry bag for boat trips and beach days
travelTips: Book Phi Phi and Similan Islands boat trips a day ahead in peak season — good operators sell out.
Avoid renting scooters without proper licensing and insurance — accident rates among tourists are high.
Stick to swimming between the red flags — Andaman Sea currents can be dangerous, especially in monsoon season.
Patong is the liveliest but most touristy base; Kata or Karon offer a calmer alternative with easy access to the same island tours.
Negotiate songthaew fares before boarding, as they are not metered.
thingsToAvoid: Jet-ski rental scams claiming pre-existing damage upon return — photograph the vehicle before and after.
Swimming during monsoon season red-flag warnings — drownings occur most years among tourists.
Overpaying at Patong's tourist-focused restaurants — better, cheaper food is a few streets inland.
Riding scooters without an international license or helmet — checkpoints do fine tourists.
Unlicensed tour operators for diving/island trips — check for proper safety certification.

name: Singapore
visaSummary: Visa-free entry for most nationalities including the US, UK, Canada, Australia and EU citizens for tourist stays of up to 30-90 days depending on nationality. Verify current requirements against your specific passport before travel.
bestTimeToVisit: February to April offers slightly lower humidity and rainfall, though Singapore's equatorial climate is warm and can see rain year-round, so any month works with proper planning.
weatherSummary: Tropical rainforest climate that is hot and humid year-round (highs 30-32°C) with frequent short afternoon showers, especially during the November-January monsoon.
etiquette: Chewing gum sale/import is restricted, and littering, jaywalking and eating on the MRT carry real fines.
Tipping is not customary and generally not expected — a service charge is usually already included.
Remove shoes when entering someone's home.
Queueing is taken seriously and orderly lines are the social norm everywhere.
Dress modestly when visiting mosques, temples, and Little India's religious sites.

localTransportGuide: The MRT subway is clean, efficient and covers virtually the entire city — an EZ-Link or contactless card works across MRT, buses and even some taxis. Grab ride-hailing is widely used, and the city is very walkable in the downtown core with covered walkways in many areas.
packingNotes: Light, breathable clothing for constant heat and humidity
A light jacket for heavily air-conditioned malls and the MRT
A compact umbrella for sudden tropical showers
Modest attire for temple and mosque visits
travelTips: Eat at hawker centers for some of the best-value, highest-quality food in the city, including Michelin-starred stalls.
Buy an EZ-Link card immediately on arrival at Changi Airport for seamless transit.
Visit Gardens by the Bay at night for the free Supertree light show in addition to daytime conservatory visits.
Changi Airport itself is a destination — Jewel Changi's indoor waterfall is worth a visit even between flights.
Book Universal Studios and major attraction tickets online in advance for time savings and occasional discounts.
thingsToAvoid: Chewing gum import/sale — it is restricted by law (personal use with no resale is generally tolerated, but don't bring large quantities).
Jaywalking, littering, or eating/drinking on the MRT — fines are strictly enforced.
Assuming tipping is expected — it is not customary and can occasionally cause confusion.
Overspending on Orchard Road malls when hawker centers and heartland neighborhoods offer better value and authenticity.
Underestimating the humidity — plan indoor/air-conditioned breaks between outdoor sightseeing.

name: Tokyo

bestTimeToVisit: Late March to April (cherry blossoms) and October to November (autumn foliage) offer mild weather and stunning seasonal scenery.
weatherSummary: Humid subtropical climate with cold winters (highs 10°C, rarely snowy) and hot, humid summers (highs 31°C); the June rainy season (tsuyu) brings frequent showers.
etiquette: Bowing is the standard greeting; a slight nod is sufficient for visitors.
Remove shoes when entering homes, some restaurants and traditional accommodations.
Do not eat or talk loudly on trains, and keep phone calls off the train entirely.
Tipping is not customary and can be seen as confusing or even rude — it's simply not done.
Slurping noodles is acceptable and even appreciated; blowing your nose in public is considered impolite.

Do not touch or chase geiko/maiko (geisha) for photos in Gion — it's illegal in parts of the district and heavily fined.
Remove shoes before entering temples, traditional ryokan and some restaurants.
Follow posted photography restrictions — some temple interiors and gardens prohibit photos entirely.
Bow slightly as a greeting and when thanking someone; a handshake is not the norm.

name: Seoul
visaSummary: Visa-free for up to 90 days for most Western passport holders (US, UK, EU, Canada, Australia) but a K-ETA (Korea Electronic Travel Authorization) is required in advance for visa-exempt travelers; some nationalities need a full visa. Check K-ETA status well before departure.
bestTimeToVisit: March to May for cherry blossoms and mild weather, or September to November for crisp air and autumn foliage.
weatherSummary: Four distinct seasons: hot, humid summers (Jun-Aug) with a July monsoon, cold dry winters (Dec-Feb) with occasional snow, and short, pleasant spring and autumn shoulder seasons.
etiquette: Bow slightly and use two hands when giving or receiving items, money, or business cards.
Remove shoes before entering homes, temples, and many traditional restaurants.
Pour drinks for others rather than yourself, and turn away slightly when drinking with elders.
Tipping is not customary and can cause confusion in restaurants and taxis.
Avoid writing a person's name in red ink, which is associated with death.

weatherSummary: Subtropical climate with hot, humid summers (May-Sep) including typhoon risk, and mild, dry winters (Dec-Feb); spring brings fog and drizzle.

bestTimeToVisit: November to February for cooler, dry weather ideal for temple exploring.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with a hot dry season (Nov-Apr, peaking in April) and a humid wet season (May-Oct) with heavy but often brief afternoon rains.
etiquette: Cover shoulders and knees when entering Angkor temple complexes.
Never climb on or touch ancient carvings or Buddha statues.
Remove hats and shoes before entering temple sanctuaries.
Do not point your feet toward Buddha images or monks.
Ask permission before photographing monks or local people closely.

name: Sydney
visaSummary: Most visitors need an electronic authorization before arrival: an ETA (Electronic Travel Authority) for passport holders including the US, Canada and Japan, or an eVisitor visa for EU and UK passport holders — both are free or low-cost and applied for online.
bestTimeToVisit: September to November (spring) or March to May (autumn) for mild temperatures and thinner crowds than summer.
weatherSummary: Temperate climate with warm, humid summers (Dec-Feb) and mild, wetter winters (Jun-Aug); seasons are reversed from the Northern Hemisphere.
etiquette: Greetings are casual and first-name based, even in business settings.
Tipping is not obligatory but 10% is appreciated for good restaurant service.
Queue politely and wait your turn on public transport and at counters.
Respect Indigenous Australian culture and any signage at sacred or culturally significant sites.
Sun safety matters — locals take UV exposure seriously even on cloudy days.

name: Queenstown

bestTimeToVisit: June to September for ski season, or December to February for summer hiking, bungy jumping and long daylight hours.
weatherSummary: Alpine climate with cold, snowy winters (Jun-Aug) suited to skiing, and mild, long-daylight summers (Dec-Feb) suited to hiking and adventure sports; seasons are reversed from the Northern Hemisphere.
etiquette: Respect Māori culture, language, and any protocols at cultural sites.
Tipping is not customary but is welcomed for outstanding service.
Sign intentions books before remote backcountry hikes for safety tracking.
Follow Leave No Trace principles in national parks and alpine areas.
Queue politely for gondolas, jet boats, and adventure activity check-ins.

name: Fiji
visaSummary: Most tourist nationalities (US, UK, EU, Australia, New Zealand, Canada) can enter visa-free for stays of up to four months, provided the passport is valid and an onward ticket can be shown.
bestTimeToVisit: May to October, the dry season, for cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and calmer seas.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with a warm, wetter season carrying cyclone risk (Nov-Apr) and a cooler, drier season with pleasant trade winds (May-Oct).
etiquette: Remove hats and sunglasses when entering a traditional village.
Wear a sulu (wraparound skirt) and cover shoulders when visiting a village.
Bring a small gift for a sevusevu (kava root offering) if invited to a village.
Ask permission before swimming or fishing near village-owned waters.
Participate respectfully in kava ceremonies, clapping once before and after drinking.

name: Bora Bora

bestTimeToVisit: May to October, the dry season, for lower humidity, calmer lagoon waters, and the best underwater visibility.
weatherSummary: Stable tropical climate with warm temperatures year-round (27-31°C); a drier, cooler season (May-Oct) and a warmer, more humid season with brief showers (Nov-Apr).
etiquette: Dress modestly when visiting Vaitape village outside the resort.
Respect the Polynesian concept of "mana," a spiritual reverence for nature and place.
Remove shoes when entering a local home.
Tipping is not customary in French Polynesia and can feel awkward if offered.
Ask before photographing local residents up close.

localTransportGuide: Renting a scooter or motorbike is the most popular way to get around Goa's beach towns, though an international driving permit and helmet are strongly advised. Uber and Ola operate in the main towns, and hired taxis are common for day trips to further-flung sights like Dudhsagar Falls.

Modest cover-up clothing for temple and church visits
Reef-safe sunscreen for beach days
Insect repellent for evenings near paddy fields and forests
travelTips: Rent a scooter for easy beach-hopping, but wear a helmet and carry an international license
Visit North Goa for nightlife and South Goa for quieter, upscale beaches
Avoid the monsoon season (Jun-Sep) for swimming due to rough seas
Bargain at flea markets but stay respectful
thingsToAvoid: Swimming during monsoon season when rip currents are dangerous and lifeguard coverage drops
Riding a scooter without a helmet or license — police checks are common
Public nudity or topless sunbathing, which is illegal
Overpaying unlicensed taxi drivers — negotiate or use ride apps

name: Kerala
visaSummary: India requires an e-Visa for most foreign nationalities, applied for online before arrival and typically valid for stays of 30-90 days depending on the category; the passport must have at least six months' validity.
bestTimeToVisit: September to March for pleasant weather, ideal for backwater cruises and hill station visits.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with a strong monsoon season (Jun-Sep) bringing heavy rainfall, and a warm, drier season (Oct-Mar) that is the most comfortable time to visit.
etiquette: Dress modestly, especially at temples, some of which restrict entry to non-Hindus.
Remove shoes before entering temples and traditional homes.
Use your right hand for eating and for giving or receiving items.
Ask permission before photographing people or religious ceremonies.
Respect Kerala's high literacy rate and progressive social norms.

localTransportGuide: Hired cars with drivers are the most practical way to cover Kerala's spread-out attractions between Kochi, Munnar, and Alleppey; local auto-rickshaws and the Ola/Uber apps work well within Kochi itself. Backwater houseboats function as both transport and accommodation for the Alleppey/Kumarakom stretch.
packingNotes: Lightweight breathable cotton clothing for humidity
A light sweater for cooler evenings in hill stations like Munnar
Modest clothing for temple visits
Mosquito repellent for backwater and forest areas
travelTips: Book a houseboat with a reputable operator and check reviews, as quality varies widely
Visit Munnar's tea plantations early morning for mist-free views
Carry cash for rural areas and small vendors where cards aren't accepted
Avoid the peak monsoon months (Jun-Aug) if backwater cruising is a priority
thingsToAvoid: Swimming at Kerala's Arabian Sea beaches without checking for rip currents and lifeguard presence
Entering Hindu temples that restrict non-Hindu visitors without checking first
Drinking tap water — stick to bottled or filtered water
Overpaying inflated "tourist price" houseboat bookings without comparing operators
